edges.description: Loss of SCYL1 scaffold function impairs COPI-mediated retrograde transport from the Golgi to the endoplasmic reticulum and disrupts Golgi architecture.
Defective retrograde trafficking and Golgi homeostasis impose secretory and ER stress on highly secretory hepatocytes and on neurons, sensitizing these cells to injury.
intermediate_mechanisms: impaired protein secretion and ER homeostasis
edges.target: Hepatocyte and Neuronal Secretory and ER Stress

edges.description: Hepatocyte vulnerability manifests as recurrent low-GGT cholestasis and acute liver failure episodes, often febrile-triggered, with cumulative injury leading to hepatic fibrosis.
edges.source: Hepatocyte and Neuronal Secretory and ER Stress
edges.target: Recurrent Hepatic Crises and Progressive Fibrosis

edges.description: Neuronal vulnerability drives later-onset, variably progressive cerebellar atrophy/ataxia and peripheral sensorimotor neuropathy.
edges.source: Hepatocyte and Neuronal Secretory and ER Stress

edges.description: Hepatic crises include recurrent low-GGT cholestasis, the laboratory clue reflected by reduced gamma-glutamyltransferase.
edges.source: Recurrent Hepatic Crises and Progressive Fibrosis

edges.description: Recurrent hepatic crises manifest as infantile-onset cholestasis.
edges.source: Recurrent Hepatic Crises and Progressive Fibrosis

edges.description: Febrile-triggered hepatic crises can present as recurrent acute liver failure.
edges.source: Recurrent Hepatic Crises and Progressive Fibrosis

edges.description: Repeated transient hepatic crises are followed by progressive hepatic fibrosis.
edges.source: Recurrent Hepatic Crises and Progressive Fibrosis

edges.description: Cerebellar neurodegeneration manifests as progressive cerebellar ataxia.
Peripheral neuronal involvement produces sensorimotor neuropathy.
Progressive neurodegeneration causes worsening gait disturbance and eventual loss of ambulation in some patients.
CALFAN neurologic involvement can include delayed gross motor development.

nodes.description: Recurrent acute liver failure with onset in infancy or early childhood, frequently triggered by febrile infections.

evidence.reference_title: SCYL1 variants cause a syndrome with low γ-glutamyl-transferase cholestasis, acute liver failure, and neurodegeneration (CALFAN).
evidence.snippet: The occurrence of SCYL1 deficiency among patients with previously undetermined infantile cholestasis or acute liver failure has not been studied

evidence.reference_title: Recurrent Acute Liver Failure in a Bahraini Child With a Novel Mutation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia-21.
evidence.snippet: He was admitted to the hospital twice by the age of two and five years due to acute hepatic failure triggered by a febrile illness.

meta.association: Causal gene; biallelic loss-of-function variants cause CALFAN syndrome

evidence.reference_title: SCYL1 variants cause a syndrome with low γ-glutamyl-transferase cholestasis, acute liver failure, and neurodegeneration (CALFAN).
evidence.snippet: Biallelic mutations in SCYL1 were recently identified as causing a syndromal disorder characterized by peripheral neuropathy, cerebellar atrophy, ataxia, and recurrent episodes of liver failure.

evidence.reference_title: Recurrent Acute Liver Failure in a Bahraini Child With a Novel Mutation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia-21.
evidence.snippet: A whole-exome sequencing (WES) test revealed that the patient has previously unreported autosomal recessive pathogenic non-sense variation c.895A>T (p.Lys299Ter) in exon 7 of the SCYL1 gene in a homozygous status.

nodes.description: Progressive cerebellar ataxia with gait impairment, often later-onset than the hepatic disease.

evidence.reference_title: SCYL1 variants cause a syndrome with low γ-glutamyl-transferase cholestasis, acute liver failure, and neurodegeneration (CALFAN).
evidence.snippet: Biallelic mutations in SCYL1 were recently identified as causing a syndromal disorder characterized by peripheral neuropathy, cerebellar atrophy, ataxia, and recurrent episodes of liver failure.

nodes.description: Cerebellar atrophy, part of the later-onset neurodegenerative phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: SCYL1 variants cause a syndrome with low γ-glutamyl-transferase cholestasis, acute liver failure, and neurodegeneration (CALFAN).
evidence.snippet: Biallelic mutations in SCYL1 were recently identified as causing a syndromal disorder characterized by peripheral neuropathy, cerebellar atrophy, ataxia, and recurrent episodes of liver failure.

nodes.description: Recurrent cholestasis with onset in infancy.

evidence.reference_title: SCYL1 variants cause a syndrome with low γ-glutamyl-transferase cholestasis, acute liver failure, and neurodegeneration (CALFAN).
evidence.snippet: The main clinical phenotype was recurrent low γ-glutamyl-transferase (GGT) cholestasis or acute liver failure with onset in infancy

nodes.description: Progressive difficulty walking, frequent falls, ultimately loss of ambulation.

evidence.reference_title: Recurrent Acute Liver Failure in a Bahraini Child With a Novel Mutation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia-21.
evidence.snippet: After the first episode of ALF, he had progressive difficulty in walking leading to frequent falls and ending with a complete inability to walk.

nodes.description: Progressive hepatic fibrosis developing despite transient liver crises; may progress to cirrhosis.

evidence.reference_title: SCYL1 variants cause a syndrome with low γ-glutamyl-transferase cholestasis, acute liver failure, and neurodegeneration (CALFAN).
evidence.snippet: Liver crises were triggered by febrile infections and were transient, but fibrosis developed.

nodes.description: Impaired Golgi-ER trafficking produces secretory stress and a proposed ER stress / unfolded protein response in hepatocytes and neurons (cerebellar Purkinje cells, motor neurons, peripheral neurons), rendering them vulnerable to injury and degeneration, particularly during the metabolic demands of febrile illness.
nodes.id: Hepatocyte and Neuronal Secretory and ER Stress

evidence.reference_title: SCYL1 variants cause a syndrome with low γ-glutamyl-transferase cholestasis, acute liver failure, and neurodegeneration (CALFAN).
evidence.snippet: The main clinical phenotype was recurrent low γ-glutamyl-transferase (GGT) cholestasis or acute liver failure with onset in infancy and a variable neurological phenotype of later onset (CALFAN syndrome).

evidence.reference_title: mTORC1 controls Golgi architecture and vesicle secretion by phosphorylation of SCYL1.
evidence.snippet: Peripheral, dephosphorylated SCYL1 causes Golgi enlargement, redistribution of early and late endosomes and increased extracellular vesicle release.

nodes.description: SCYL1 deficiency disrupts COPI-coated vesicle-mediated retrograde transport from the Golgi to the endoplasmic reticulum and alters Golgi organization. Mechanistic cell-biology work shows that the mTORC1-controlled phosphorylation status of SCYL1 (Ser754) regulates Golgi architecture, endolysosomal distribution, and extracellular vesicle secretion, and that loss-of-function of SCYL1 underlies CALFAN syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: mTORC1 controls Golgi architecture and vesicle secretion by phosphorylation of SCYL1.
evidence.snippet: Here, we characterize the N-terminal kinase-like protein SCYL1 as a Golgi-localized target through which mTORC1 controls organelle distribution and extracellular vesicle secretion in breast cancer cells.

evidence.reference_title: mTORC1 controls Golgi architecture and vesicle secretion by phosphorylation of SCYL1.
evidence.snippet: It may also explain the pathophysiology underlying human genetic diseases such as CALFAN syndrome, which is caused by loss-of-function of SCYL1.

evidence.reference_title: Intracellular Trafficking Defects in Congenital Intestinal and Hepatic Diseases.
evidence.snippet: We give examples of how intracellular trafficking hubs, such as the apical recycling endosome system, the trans-Golgi network, lysosomes, or the Golgi-to-endoplasmic reticulum transport are involved in the pathomechanism and lead to disease.

nodes.description: Characteristic low or normal serum gamma-glutamyltransferase (GGT) in the setting of cholestasis, an important diagnostic clue distinguishing CALFAN from high-GGT cholestatic disorders.

evidence.reference_title: SCYL1 variants cause a syndrome with low γ-glutamyl-transferase cholestasis, acute liver failure, and neurodegeneration (CALFAN).
evidence.snippet: The main clinical phenotype was recurrent low γ-glutamyl-transferase (GGT) cholestasis or acute liver failure with onset in infancy and a variable neurological phenotype of later onset (CALFAN syndrome).

nodes.description: Delayed acquisition of gross motor milestones reported in genetically confirmed CALFAN patients (e.g., delayed independent walking).

evidence.reference_title: Recurrent Acute Liver Failure in a Bahraini Child With a Novel Mutation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia-21.
evidence.snippet: The patient had delayed gross motor development as he started to walk at 20 months of age.

nodes.description: Peripheral motor-sensory (sensorimotor) neuropathy, part of the progressive neurodegenerative phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: SCYL1 variants cause a syndrome with low γ-glutamyl-transferase cholestasis, acute liver failure, and neurodegeneration (CALFAN).
evidence.snippet: Biallelic mutations in SCYL1 were recently identified as causing a syndromal disorder characterized by peripheral neuropathy, cerebellar atrophy, ataxia, and recurrent episodes of liver failure.

nodes.description: Later-onset and variably progressive neurodegeneration including cerebellar atrophy with ataxia and peripheral sensorimotor (motor-sensory) neuropathy. Neurologic decline may continue independently of hepatic disease and can persist after liver transplantation.

evidence.reference_title: SCYL1 variants cause a syndrome with low γ-glutamyl-transferase cholestasis, acute liver failure, and neurodegeneration (CALFAN).
evidence.snippet: Biallelic mutations in SCYL1 were recently identified as causing a syndromal disorder characterized by peripheral neuropathy, cerebellar atrophy, ataxia, and recurrent episodes of liver failure.

evidence.reference_title: Recurrent Acute Liver Failure in a Bahraini Child With a Novel Mutation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia-21.
evidence.snippet: After the first episode of ALF, he had progressive difficulty in walking leading to frequent falls and ending with a complete inability to walk.

nodes.description: Episodic low/normal-GGT cholestasis and acute liver failure, frequently triggered by febrile infections, with onset in infancy. Crises are often transient with inter-episodic biochemical recovery, but hepatic fibrosis develops and can progress to cirrhosis in some patients.
nodes.id: Recurrent Hepatic Crises and Progressive Fibrosis

evidence.reference_title: SCYL1 variants cause a syndrome with low γ-glutamyl-transferase cholestasis, acute liver failure, and neurodegeneration (CALFAN).
evidence.snippet: Liver crises were triggered by febrile infections and were transient, but fibrosis developed.

evidence.reference_title: Recurrent Acute Liver Failure in a Bahraini Child With a Novel Mutation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia-21.
evidence.snippet: He was admitted to the hospital twice by the age of two and five years due to acute hepatic failure triggered by a febrile illness.

nodes.description: Biallelic loss-of-function variants in SCYL1 abolish or reduce functional SCY1-like pseudokinase 1 protein. SCYL1 is an N-terminal kinase-like scaffold that operates at the Golgi apparatus in COPI-dependent vesicular trafficking.

evidence.reference_title: SCYL1 variants cause a syndrome with low γ-glutamyl-transferase cholestasis, acute liver failure, and neurodegeneration (CALFAN).
evidence.snippet: Functional studies emphasize that SCYL1 deficiency is linked to impaired intracellular trafficking.
